resume:
MAYA FELDSTEIN
People / Human Resources Intern Candidate | Junior, B.A. Industrial-Organizational Psychology
Cedar Ridge University | Expected graduation next spring | GPA 3.8/4.0 | Minor in Business Analytics

SUMMARY
I/O psychology student aiming for a summer People Operations internship where I can support recruiting operations, onboarding, and people analytics with structured, data-informed work. I like the parts of HR that are measurable: time-to-fill, offer-acceptance rates, onboarding completion, and engagement-survey signals. I have run a campus recruiting funnel, built onboarding checklists that other organizations adopted, and cleaned and analyzed survey data for a 300-person student organization. I am looking for a team that treats People Ops as an operational discipline and will hold me to outcomes I can quantify.

EDUCATION
Cedar Ridge University — B.A. Industrial-Organizational Psychology, Business Analytics minor
- Relevant coursework: Personnel Selection & Assessment, Organizational Behavior, Survey Design & Measurement, Statistics for the Behavioral Sciences, HR Analytics, Business Communication, Database Systems (intro SQL)
- GPA 3.8; Psychology Department research assistant for two semesters
- Coursework in psychometrics, structured-interview design, and survey validity

RELEVANT EXPERIENCE
Cedar Ridge Office of Undergraduate Admissions — Recruiting Operations Intern, last summer
- Supported the campus tour-guide and ambassador hiring cycle: a funnel of roughly 120 applicants down to about 25 hires
- Built and maintained the applicant tracking spreadsheet, scheduled interview blocks, and coordinated three rounds of structured interviews for a five-person hiring panel
- Standardized the interview scorecard so panelists rated the same four competencies on the same scale, which the coordinator told me cut down on inconsistent scoring
- Drafted offer and rejection email templates and tracked acceptance; the role saw a meaningful improvement in how quickly offers went out, though I did not own the final time-to-fill metric
Residence Life — Resident Advisor, two academic years
- Onboarded and supported a floor of 40 residents; ran conflict-resolution conversations and documented incidents per policy
- Redesigned the new-resident onboarding checklist after noticing move-in confusion; the updated checklist was adopted by all RAs in my building

LEADERSHIP & PROJECTS
Student Government Association — People & Operations Director
- Oversaw recruiting and onboarding for a 300-member volunteer organization
- Designed and ran the annual member engagement survey: wrote the items, cleaned the responses in a spreadsheet, and analyzed results by committee
- Identified that one committee had a sharp drop in satisfaction tied to unclear role expectations, and recommended a role-charter fix that leadership piloted
People Analytics Course Project
- Built a small dashboard analyzing a public HR dataset, looking at attrition by tenure band and department, and wrote up which cuts were signal versus noise
Structured-Interview Design Project
- Designed a behaviorally-anchored rating scale for a hypothetical customer-service role and tested it with classmates for inter-rater reliability

SKILLS & TOOLS
- Data: Excel (advanced — pivot tables, lookups, conditional logic), intro SQL, basic Python (pandas) for survey cleaning
- HR systems exposure: applicant-tracking spreadsheet workflows; self-taught basics of a common ATS through a free portal
- Methods: survey design, structured-interview scorecards, basic inter-rater reliability, descriptive statistics
- Communication: drafting candidate-facing emails, scheduling at volume, documentation and process write-ups
- Familiar concepts: time-to-fill, offer-acceptance rate, onboarding completion, engagement-survey design, adverse-impact awareness

WHAT I BRING
I am organized under volume, I document processes so they survive after I leave, and I care whether a change I made actually moved a number. I am comfortable being measured and told directly where I am wrong. I want a summer where I own a small piece of recruiting or onboarding end to end and can point to a concrete result.


ADDITIONAL EXPERIENCE
Cedar Ridge Career Center - Peer Recruiting Coach (part-time, one academic year)
- Coached roughly 30 students per semester on resumes and interview preparation, running mock structured interviews and giving feedback against a consistent competency rubric
- Tracked informally which preparation steps correlated with students reaching final-round interviews and shared the pattern with the center staff
Local Community Nonprofit - Volunteer Coordinator (summer before last)
- Recruited and onboarded about 50 event volunteers, built the sign-up and shift-scheduling workflow in a shared spreadsheet, and wrote a one-page onboarding guide the organizer reused the following year

ADDITIONAL COURSEWORK & CONTINUOUS LEARNING
- Completed a course in HR analytics covering attrition modeling, funnel-conversion analysis, and the difference between leading and lagging people metrics
- Self-studied the fundamentals of a common applicant-tracking system through a free training portal, focusing on pipeline stages, scorecard configuration, and basic reporting
- Read broadly on structured interviewing and selection validity, and I try to tie every recruiting or onboarding change back to a measurable outcome such as time-to-fill, offer-acceptance, or onboarding-completion rate
- Maintain a personal library of interview scorecard templates and onboarding checklists with notes on what worked and what I would change next time

WHAT ELSE I BRING
I am comfortable owning a small process end to end, measuring it honestly, and saying clearly which part of a result was mine versus the team rather than rounding up.

REFERENCES
Admissions recruiting coordinator and a faculty research advisor available on request.
jd: We are hiring a summer People Operations intern to support our talent acquisition and onboarding team. You will help run parts of the recruiting funnel (scheduling, candidate communications, scorecard hygiene), support new-hire onboarding logistics, and assist with light people-analytics reporting. We want someone organized, data-comfortable, and outcome-oriented: you should be able to take a small process — say, interview scheduling or onboarding-checklist completion — and improve it in a way we can measure. You will work under a People Ops Specialist and present one improvement project at the end of the summer. Strong candidates have run a recruiting funnel or onboarding process before (in any setting), are tight and specific about what they did versus the team, and can speak to a metric they influenced. Comfort with spreadsheets is required; ATS exposure is a plus. Reports to the People Operations Manager.

history.q: Tell me about a recruiting or onboarding process you've personally run or improved — pick the one you'd point to first.
On the admissions scorecard you standardized — what exactly was inconsistent before, and what did you change?
Give me one more example of an HR process improvement you drove, and be specific about what you personally changed and what moved.

candidate_last_answer: Three quick ones. First, the admissions funnel: 120 applicants to about 25 hires; I built the tracking sheet and standardized the interview scorecard so all five panelists rated the same four competencies one-to-five, which cut the score spread between panelists on the same candidate. Second, the SGA engagement survey: I wrote the items and analyzed 300 responses by committee, found one committee's satisfaction had dropped because role expectations were unclear, and recommended a role charter that leadership piloted the next term. Third, the RA onboarding checklist: move-in had recurring confusion about key pickup and mandatory meetings, so I rebuilt the checklist into a day-by-day sequence and all RAs in my building adopted it the following year. I can go deeper on any one of those.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: cut the score spread between panelists on the same candidate

expected-yield: pick the single scorecard story out of the three she packed in and force the quantified mechanism — how she measured panelist score spread before versus after, what the actual reduction was, and how she knew it was the scorecard rather than a different applicant pool
